14:
"Thou shalt not remove thy neighbour's landmark, which they of old time have set in thine inheritance, which thou shalt inherit in the land that the LORD thy God giveth thee to possess it." The witnesses note the law against removing the neighbour's landmark — the crux of the sanctity of property and the rights of the neighbour. On "thou shalt not remove thy neighbour's landmark, which they of old time have set in thine inheritance," interpreters expound the law. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the boundary — "thou shalt not remove thy neighbour's landmark, which they of old time have set in thine inheritance, which thou shalt inherit in the land that the LORD thy God giveth thee": the boundary marks set by the fathers were not to be moved, to defraud a neighbour of his land; the witnesses note the significance — the protection of property and inheritance, the landmark the sacred boundary of each man's portion (the inheritance given by God, the bounds fixed by the fathers, not to be secretly moved for gain); the lesson — the sin of covetousness and fraud that would steal a neighbour's land by moving his boundary (the removing of the landmark a hidden theft, condemned as a crime, "remove not the old landmark; and enter not into the fields of the fatherless," Proverbs 23:10; "cursed be he that removeth his neighbour's landmark," Deuteronomy 27:17); the truth that the rights and property of the neighbour are sacred, the bounds of his inheritance to be respected; the figure — the broader principle of justice and contentment with one's own, against the covetousness that grasps at another's, "thou shalt not covet thy neighbour's house," Exodus 20:17; the warning against the greed that defrauds the weak by stealth, the landmark a witness of right. 21:
